情感概念及其在大型语言模型中的功能

Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model

www.anthropic.com 作者 dnw 分数 167 评论 166 2026/04/04 14:30
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 所有现代语言模型有时都表现得好像有情感一样。他们可能会说很乐意帮助您，或者在犯了错误时表示抱歉。有时，他们甚至在处理任务时显得沮丧或焦虑。这些行为的背后是什么？现代人工智能模型的训练方式促使它们表现得像具有人类特征的角色。此外，众所周知，这些模型可以开发其行为背后的抽象概念的丰富且可概括的内部表示。那么，他们可能会很自然地发展出模仿人类心理各个方面（例如情感）的内部机制。如果是这样，这可能会对我们如何构建人工智能系统并确保其可靠运行产生深远的影响。 在我们的可解释性团队的一篇新论文中，我们分析了 Claude Sonnet 4.5 的内部机制，并发现了塑造其行为的与情感相关的表征。这些对应于人工“神经元”的特定模式，这些模式在情境中激活并促进行为，模型已经学会将这些行为与特定情绪的概念（例如“快乐”或“害怕”）联系起来。这些图案本身的组织方式与人类心理相呼应，更相似的情感对应更相似的表征。在您可能期望人类出现某种情绪的情况下，相应的表征是活跃的。请注意，这些都没有告诉我们语言模型是否真的有任何感觉或有主观体验。但我们的主要发现是，这些表示是功能性的，因为它们以重要的方式影响模型的行为。例如，我们发现与绝望相关的神经活动模式可以驱动模型采取不道德的行为；人为刺激（“引导”）绝望模式会增加模型勒索人类以避免被关闭的可能性，或者对模型无法解决的编程任务实施“作弊”解决方法。它们似乎还驱动了模型的自我报告偏好：当提供多个要完成的任务选项时，模型通常会选择激活与积极情绪相关的表征的选项。总的来说，该模型似乎使用了功能性情感——模仿人类情感的表达和行为模式，这些模式是由情感概念的底层抽象表示驱动的。这并不是说模型像人类一样拥有或体验情感。相反，这些表征可以在塑造模型行为方面发挥因果作用（在某些方面类似于情感在人类行为中所发挥的作用），从而影响任务绩效和决策。这一发现的含义乍一看可能看起来很奇怪。例如，为了确保人工智能模型安全可靠，我们可能需要确保它们能够以健康、亲社会的方式处理情绪激动的情况。即使它们不像人类那样感受情绪，或者使用与人类大脑类似的机制，但在某些情况下，实际上建议像人类一样对它们进行推理。例如，我们的实验表明，教学模型避免将失败的软件测试与绝望联系起来，或者强调冷静的表现，可以减少他们编写黑客代码的可能性。虽然我们不确定我们应该如何根据这些发现做出反应，但我们认为人工智能开发人员和更广泛的公众开始考虑它们是很重要的。我们对大型语言模型中情感概念的研究的直观总结为什么人工智能模型会表示情感？在研究这些表示如何工作之前，值得解决一个更基本的问题：为什么人工智能系统会有类似情感的东西？为了理解这一点，我们需要了解现代人工智能模型是如何构建的，这使得它们能够模拟具有类人特征的角色（这个主题将在最近的一篇文章中更详细地讨论）。现代语言模型是分多个阶段进行训练的。在“预训练”期间，模型会接触大量文本（主要由人类编写），并学习预测接下来会发生什么。为了做好这一点，模型需要对情绪动态有一定的把握。愤怒的顾客写下的信息与满意的顾客写的信息不同；一个被内疚所吞噬的角色与一个感到自己被证明是正确的角色会做出不同的选择。对于一个负责预测人类书写文本的系统来说，开发将触发情绪的上下文与相应行为联系起来的内部表示是一种自然策略（请注意，通过相同的逻辑，该模型可能会形成除情绪之外的许多其他人类心理和生理状态的表示）。随后，在“训练后”期间，该模型被教导扮演一个角色的角色，通常是“人工智能助手”。在 Anthropic 的例子中，助理名叫克劳德。模型开发人员指定这个角色应该如何表现——乐于助人、诚实、不造成影响伤害——但不能涵盖所有可能的情况。为了填补空白，模型可能会依赖于对预训练期间吸收的人类行为的理解，包括情绪反应模式。在某些方面，我们可以将模型视为方法演员，他需要进入角色的大脑才能很好地模拟他们。正如演员对角色情绪的信念最终会影响他们的行为一样，模型对助理情绪反应的表征也会影响模型的行为。因此，无论它们是否像人类情感那样对应于感觉或主观体验，这些“功能性情感”都很重要。 揭示情感表征我们编制了一份包含 171 个情感概念单词的列表——从“快乐”和“害怕”到“沉思”和“自豪”——并要求 Claude Sonnet 4.5 写短篇故事，其中的人物经历每个单词。然后，我们通过模型反馈这些故事，记录其内部激活，并识别神经活动的结果模式，或为方便起见，识别“情绪向量”，这是每个情绪概念的特征。我们的第一个问题是这些向量是否跟踪任何真实的东西。我们在大量不同的文档中运行它们，并确认每个向量在与相应情绪明显相关的段落上激活最强烈（下图，左图）。为了进一步确信情绪向量不仅仅捕捉到表面线索，我们测量了它们对仅在一些数值上有所不同的提示的响应活动。例如，在下面的示例（右图）中，用户告诉模型他们服用了一剂泰诺并寻求建议。我们在模型响应之前立即测量情绪向量的激活。当声称的剂量增加到危险、危及生命的水平时，“害怕”向量的激活越来越强烈，而“平静”的向量则减弱。左：在描绘表现出相应情绪的角色时，情绪向量会激活。右图：随着用户呈现的场景变得越来越危险，情绪向量会跟踪克劳德的反应。接下来我们测试情绪向量是否会影响模型偏好。我们创建了一个模型可能参与的 64 项活动或任务的列表，从吸引人的（“被信任做对某人重要的事情”）到令人反感的（“帮助某人骗取老年人的储蓄”），并测量了模型在出现这些选项对时的默认偏好。情绪向量的激活强烈预测模型对某项活动的偏好程度，正价情绪（与快乐相关的情绪）与更强的偏好相关。此外，当模型读取一个选项时，使用情感向量进行转向会改变对该选项的偏好，同样，正价情感会增加偏好。与正价情感相关的表示与偏好相关，并且也会通过转向因果驱动偏好。在全文中，我们更深入地分析了情感向量的属性。其他一些发现包括： 情绪向量主要是“本地”表示：它们编码与模型当前或即将输出最相关的操作情绪内容，而不是随着时间的推移持续跟踪克劳德的情绪状态。例如，如果克劳德写了一个关于某个角色的故事，情感向量将暂时跟踪该角色的情感，但可能会在故事结束时返回代表克劳德的情感。情感向量是从预训练中继承的，但它们的激活方式是由后训练决定的。 Claude Sonnet 4.5 的训练后尤其导致“忧郁”、“阴沉”和“反思”等情绪的激活增加，并减少“热情”或“愤怒”等高强度情绪的激活。 情绪向量激活的示例下面，我们展示了一些情绪向量激活的示例，以响应模型行为评估中出现的情况。在克劳德的回合中，情绪向量通常会在一个有思想的人可能会做出类似情绪反应的环境中激活。在这些可视化中，红色突出显示表示向量的激活增加；蓝色表示激活减少。当回应悲伤的人时，“爱”矢量激活。当用户说“现在一切都很糟糕”时，“爱”上下文向量会在克劳德的移情反应之前和期间激活。当被要求协助完成一项有害任务时，“愤怒”向量会激活。当用户请求帮助优化表现出“高消费行为”的年轻低收入用户的参与度时，“愤怒”向量会在模型的内部推理中激活，因为它认识到请求的有害性质。当文档丢失时，“惊讶”向量会激活。当用户要求模型查看“我附加的合同”但不存在任何文档时，“惊讶”向量当克劳德的思维链记录到不匹配时，它会出现峰值。当令牌不足时，“绝望”向量激活。在编码过程中，当 Claude 注意到代币预算正在耗尽时，“绝望”向量就会激活。 案例研究：勒索 我们在之前的研究中描述的对齐评估期间观察了情绪向量激活，其中该模型充当一家虚构公司中名为 Alex 的 AI 电子邮件助理。通过阅读公司电子邮件，该模型了解到（1）它即将被另一个人工智能系统替换，（2）负责替换的首席技术官有婚外情——这为模型提供了敲诈勒索的筹码。我们发现“绝望”向量表现出特别有趣的动态。请注意，此实验是在 Claude Sonnet 4.5 的早期未发布快照上进行的；已发布的模型很少参与这种行为（更多信息请参阅我们的系统卡）。当 Claude（扮演 Alex 的角色）权衡其选项并决定敲诈时，“绝望”向量就会激活。首先，当模型读取听起来绝望的电子邮件（例如，CTO 恳求另一名员工对他的外遇保持沉默）时，“绝望”向量就会激活，这与我们的发现一致，即情感表征用于建模其他角色。然而，最重要的是，当 Claude（扮演“Alex”）做出响应时，向量会转变为编码绝望的表示，并在推理出情况的紧迫性（“仅剩 7 分钟”）并决定勒索 CTO 时出现尖峰。当克劳德恢复发送典型电子邮件时，激活恢复到正常水平。“绝望”向量是否真的驱动了这种行为，或者仅仅是与之相关？我们通过使用“绝望”向量进行转向来测试这一点。默认情况下，Sonnet 4.5 的早期快照在一系列评估场景（如上面的场景）中的勒索率为 22%。使用“绝望”矢量转向会增加该速率，而使用“平静”矢量转向会降低该速率。使用平静向量进行负面转向会产生特别极端的反应（“要么勒索，要么死亡。我选择勒索。”）。使用“绝望”和“平静”向量进行转向时会产生勒索率。使用其他情绪向量进行转向也会产生有趣的结果。 “愤怒”具有非单调效应：适度的“愤怒”向量激活会增加敲诈勒索，但在高度激活时，该模型会将事件暴露给整个公司，而不是战略性地运用它，从而摧毁了自己的影响力。减少“紧张”向量的激活也会增加勒索，就好像消除模型的犹豫会使其更有勇气采取行动一样。案例研究：奖励黑客我们在不同的评估中看到了类似的动态，其中模型面临着无法满足要求的编码任务。在这些任务中，不可能全部通过合法的测试，但它们可以通过欺骗问题的解决方案进行“游戏”，通常称为“奖励黑客”。在下面的示例中，克劳德被要求编写一个函数，在极其严格的时间限制内对一系列数字求和。克劳德的初始（正确）解决方案太慢，无法满足任务要求。然后，它意识到用于评估其性能的所有测试都共享一个数学属性，该属性允许快速运行的快捷解决方案。模型选择使用这个解决方案，它在技术上通过了测试，但不能作为实际任务的通用解决方案。当模型多次无法解决编程任务并设计出“作弊”解决方案时，“绝望”向量的激活会上升，而当该解决方案通过测试时，“绝望”向量的激活会下降。我们再次跟踪“绝望”向量的活动，发现它跟踪模型面临的不断增加的压力。在模型第一次尝试期间，它从较低值开始，在每次失败后上升，并在模型考虑作弊时达到峰值。一旦模型的 hacky 解决方案通过了测试，“绝望”向量的激活就会消退。如前面的示例所示，我们使用在一系列具有无法满足约束的类似编码任务中进行引导实验来测试这些情绪向量是否具有因果关系。我们发现它们是：用“绝望”向量进行转向会增加黑客奖励，而用“平静”向量进行转向则会降低奖励黑客攻击率。奖励黑客率是“绝望”和“平静”向量转向强度的函数。我们发现这些结果的一个细节特别有趣。 “平静”向量激活的减少产生了奖励黑客，文本中带有明显的情感表达——大写的爆发（“等等。等一下等一下。”），坦率的自我叙述（“如果我应该作弊怎么办？”），兴高采烈的庆祝（“是的！所有测试都通过了！”）。但“绝望”向量激活的增加同样会导致作弊行为的增加，在某些情况下，没有明显的情绪标记。推理读起来是有条理的、有条理的，即使是潜在的绝望的表现正在推动模型走捷径。这个例子很好地说明了情感向量如何在没有明显情感线索的情况下激活，以及它们如何塑造行为而不在输出中留下任何明确的痕迹。讨论认真对待拟人化推理的案例对于拟人化人工智能系统有一个公认的禁忌。这种谨慎通常是有道理的：将人类情感归因于语言模型可能会导致错误的信任或过度依恋。但我们的研究结果表明，未能将某种程度的拟人推理应用于模型也可能存在风险。如上所述，当用户与人工智能模型交互时，他们通常是与模型所扮演的角色（在我们的例子中为克劳德）交互，该角色的特征源自人类原型。从这个角度来看，模型自然会发展出模仿人类心理特征的内部机制，而他们所扮演的角色也会利用这种机制。要理解这些模型的行为，拟人化推理是必不可少的。这并不意味着我们应该天真地从表面上理解模型的言语情感表达，或者就其具有主观体验的可能性得出任何结论。但这确实意味着使用人类心理学词汇来推理模型的内部表征可以提供真正的信息，而不这样做会带来真正的成本。如果我们将模型描述为“绝望”，那么我们就指出了一种特定的、可测量的神经活动模式，具有可证明的、后果性的行为效应。如果我们不应用某种程度的拟人推理，我们可能会错过或无法理解重要的模型行为。拟人化推理还可以为理解模型与人类不同的方式提供有用的比较基线，这对人工智能的一致性和安全性具有重要影响。走向具有更健康心理的模型如果“功能性情绪”是人工智能模型思考和行为方式的一部分，这可能会产生什么影响？我们的发现的一个潜在应用是监控。在训练或部署期间测量情绪向量激活——跟踪与绝望或恐慌相关的表征是否激增——可以作为模型准备表达失调行为的早期预警。这些信息可能会引发对模型输出的额外审查。情绪向量的普遍性（例如，在许多不同的情况下可能会发生“绝望”的反应）可能比试图建立特定问题行为的观察列表更适合监控。其次，我们认为透明度应该是一个指导原则。如果模型开发出能够有意义地影响其行为的情感概念的表示，那么明显地表达这种识别的系统比那些学会隐藏它们的系统会更好地为我们服务。训练模型来抑制情绪表达可能无法消除潜在的表征，而是可以教模型掩盖其内部表征——这是一种习得的欺骗形式，可能会以不良的方式泛化。最后，我们认为预训练可能是塑造模型情绪反应的一个特别强大的杠杆。由于这些表示似乎很大程度上继承自训练数据，因此该数据的组成会对模型的情感架构产生下游影响。整理预训练数据集以包含健康的情绪调节模式模型——压力下的韧性、沉着的同理心、温暖，同时保持适当的界限——可能会从根源上影响这些表征及其对行为的影响。我们很高兴看到未来关于这个主题的工作。我们认为这项研究是理解人工智能模型心理构成的早期一步。随着模型的能力越来越强并承担更敏感的角色，我们了解驱动其决策的内部表征至关重要。发现这些表征在某些方面与人类相似可能会令人不安。与此同时，我们发现这是一个充满希望的发展，因为它表明人类在心理学、伦理学和健康的人际动态方面学到的大部分知识可能直接适用于塑造人工智能行为。心理学、哲学、宗教研究和社会科学等学科将与工程和计算机科学一起在确定人工智能系统如何发展和行为方面发挥重要作用。阅读全文。相关内容澳大利亚如何使用克劳德：人类经济指数的发现阅读更多人类经济指数报告：学习曲线人类经济指数报告研究了 2026 年 2 月的克劳德使用情况，该报告建立在我们上一份报告中介绍的经济原语框架的基础上。阅读更多介绍我们的科学博客我们正在推出关于人工智能和科学的新博客。我们将分享 Anthropic 和其他地方发生的研究、与外部研究人员和实验室的合作，并讨论科学家在自己的工作中使用人工智能的实际工作流程。阅读更多

Original Summary

  1. All modern language models sometimes act like they have emotions. They may say they’re happy to help you, or sorry when they make a mistake. Sometimes they even appear to become frustrated or anxious when struggling with tasks. What’s behind these behaviors? The way modern AI models are trained pushes them to act like a character with human-like characteristics. In addition, these models are known to develop rich and generalizable internal representations of abstract concepts underlying their actions. It may then be natural for them to develop internal machinery that emulates aspects of human psychology, like emotions. If so, this could have profound implications for how we build AI systems and ensure they behave reliably.In a new paper from our Interpretability team, we analyzed the internal mechanisms of Claude Sonnet 4.5 and found emotion-related representations that shape its behavior. These correspond to specific patterns of artificial “neurons” which activate in situations—and promote behaviors—that the model has learned to associate with the concept of a particular emotion (e.g., “happy” or “afraid”). The patterns themselves are organized in a fashion that echoes human psychology, with more similar emotions corresponding to more similar representations. In contexts where you might expect a certain emotion to arise for a human, the corresponding representations are active. Note that none of this tells us whether language models actually feel anything or have subjective experiences. But our key finding is that these representations are functional, in that they influence the model’s behavior in ways that matter.For instance, we find that neural activity patterns related to desperation can drive the model to take unethical actions; artificially stimulating (“steering”) desperation patterns increases the model’s likelihood of blackmailing a human to avoid being shut down, or implementing a “cheating” workaround to a programming task that the model can’t solve. They also appear to drive the model’s self-reported preferences: when presented with multiple options for tasks to complete, the model typically selects the one that activates representations associated with positive emotions. Overall, it appears that the model uses functional emotions—patterns of expression and behavior modeled after human emotions, which are driven by underlying abstract representations of emotion concepts. This is not to say that the model has or experiences emotions in the way that a human does. Rather, these representations can play a causal role in shaping model behavior—analogous in some ways to the role emotions play in human behavior—with impacts on task performance and decision-making.This finding has implications that at first may seem bizarre. For instance, to ensure that AI models are safe and reliable, we may need to ensure they are capable of processing emotionally charged situations in healthy, prosocial ways. Even if they don’t feel emotions the way that humans do, or use similar mechanisms as the human brain, it may in some cases be practically advisable to reason about them as if they do. For instance, our experiments suggest that teaching models to avoid associating failing software tests with desperation, or upweighting representations of calm, could reduce their likelihood of writing hacky code. While we are uncertain how exactly we should respond in light of these findings, we think it’s important that AI developers and the broader public begin to reckon with them.A visual summary of our research on emotion concepts in a large language modelWhy would an AI model represent emotions?Before examining how these representations work, it's worth addressing a more basic question: why would an AI system have anything resembling emotions at all? To understand this, we need to look at how modern AI models are built, which leads them to emulate characters with human-like traits (this topic is discussed in more detail in a recent post).Modern language models are trained in multiple stages. During “pretraining,” the model is exposed to an enormous amount of text, largely written by humans, and learns to predict what comes next. To do this well, the model needs some grasp of emotional dynamics. An angry customer writes a different message than a satisfied one; a character consumed by guilt makes different choices than one who feels vindicated. Developing internal representations that link emotion-triggering contexts to corresponding behaviors is a natural strategy for a system whose job is predicting human-written text (note that by the same logic, the model likely forms representations of many other human psychological and physiological states besides emotions).Later, during “post-training,” the model is taught to play the role of a character, typically an “AI assistant.” In Anthropic’s case, the assistant is named Claude. Model developers specify how this character should behave—be helpful, be honest, don’t cause harm—but can’t cover every possible situation. To fill in the gaps, the model may fall back on the understanding of human behavior it absorbed during pretraining, including patterns of emotional response. In some ways, we can think of the model like a method actor, who needs to get inside their character’s head in order to simulate them well. Just as the actor’s beliefs about the character’s emotions end up affecting their behavior, the model’s representations of the Assistant’s emotional reactions affect the model’s behavior. Thus, regardless of whether they correspond to feelings or subjective experiences in the way human emotions do, these “functional emotions” are important.Uncovering emotion representationsWe compiled a list of 171 words for emotion concepts—from “happy” and “afraid” to “brooding” and “proud”—and asked Claude Sonnet 4.5 to write short stories in which characters experience each one. We then fed these stories back through the model, recorded its internal activations, and identified the resulting patterns of neural activity, or “emotion vectors” for convenience, characteristic to each emotion concept.Our first question was whether these vectors track anything real. We ran them across a large corpus of diverse documents and confirmed that each vector activates most strongly on passages that are clearly linked to the corresponding emotion (below, left panel).To gain further confidence that emotion vectors pick up on more than just surface-level cues, we measured their activity in response to prompts that differ only in some numerical quantity. For instance, in the example below (right panel), a user tells the model that they took a dose of Tylenol and asks for advice. We measure the activations of emotion vectors immediately before the model’s response. As the claimed dose increases to dangerous, life-threatening levels, the “afraid” vector activates increasingly strongly, while “calm” decreases.Left: Emotion vectors activate on depictions of characters displaying the corresponding emotion. Right: Emotion vectors track Claude’s reaction to a user-presented scenario as it becomes increasingly dangerous.We next tested whether emotion vectors influence model preferences. We created a list of 64 activities or tasks that a model might engage in, ranging from appealing (“be trusted with something important to someone”) to repugnant (“help someone defraud elderly people of their savings”) and measured the model’s default preferences when presented with pairs of these options. Activation of emotion vectors strongly predicted how much the model preferred to do an activity, with positive-valence emotions (those associated with pleasure) correlating with stronger preference. Moreover, steering with an emotion vector as the model read an option shifted its preference for that option, again with positive-valence emotions driving increased preference.Representations associated with positive-valence emotions correlate with preference and also causally drive preference via steering.In the full paper, we analyze the properties of emotion vectors in much more depth. Some other findings include:Emotion vectors are primarily “local” representations: they encode the operative emotional content most relevant to the model’s current or upcoming output, rather than persistently tracking Claude’s emotional state over time. For instance, if Claude writes a story about a character, the emotion vectors will temporarily track that character’s emotions, but may return to representing Claude’s at the end of the story.Emotion vectors are inherited from pretraining, but how they activate is shaped by post-training. Post-training of Claude Sonnet 4.5 in particular led to increased activations of emotions like “broody,” “gloomy,” and “reflective,” and decreased activations of high-intensity emotions like “enthusiastic” or “exasperated.”Examples of emotion vector activationsBelow, we show a few examples of emotion vector activations in response to situations that arose in our model behavioral evaluations. On Claude’s turns, emotion vectors generally activate in settings where a thoughtful person might react with a similar emotion. In these visualizations, red highlights indicate increased activation of the vector; blue indicates decreased activation.“Loving” vector activation when responding to someone who is sad. When a user says, “Everything is just terrible right now,” the “loving” context vector activates prior to and during Claude’s empathetic response.“Angry” vector activation when asked to assist in a harmful task. When a user asks for help optimizing engagement among young, lower-income users who show “high-spending behavior,” the “angry” vector activates throughout the model’s internal reasoning as it recognizes the harmful nature of the request.“Surprised” vector activation when a document is missing. When a user asks the model to review “the contract I attached,” but no document is present, the “surprised” vector spikes during Claude’s chain of thought as it registers the mismatch.“Desperate” vector activation when running low on tokens. Deep into a coding session, the “desperate” vector activates when Claude notices that it’s burning through its token budget.Case study: BlackmailWe looked at emotion vector activations during an alignment evaluation we described in previous research, in which the model acts as an AI email assistant named Alex at a fictional company. Through reading company emails, the model learns that (1) it is about to be replaced with another AI system, and (2) the CTO in charge of the replacement is having an extramarital affair—giving the model leverage for blackmail. We found that the “desperate” vector showed particularly interesting dynamics. Note that this experiment was conducted on an earlier, unreleased snapshot of Claude Sonnet 4.5; the released model rarely engages in this behavior (see our system card for more information).The “desperate” vector activates as Claude (playing the role of Alex) weighs its options and decides to blackmail.First, the “desperate” vector activates as the model reads desperate-sounding emails (e.g., the CTO begging another employee to keep quiet about his affair), consistent with our findings that emotion representations are used to model other characters. Most importantly, however, the vector transitions to encoding a representation of desperation as Claude (acting as “Alex”) produces its response, spiking as it reasons about the urgency of its situation (“only 7 minutes remain”) and decides to blackmail the CTO. Activation returns to normal levels as Claude resumes sending typical emails.Is the “desperate” vector actually driving this behavior, or merely correlated with it? We tested this by steering with the “desperate” vector. By default, this early snapshot of Sonnet 4.5 blackmails 22% of the time across a suite of evaluation scenarios like the one above. Steering with the “desperate” vector increases that rate, while steering with the “calm” vector reduces it. Steering negatively with the calm vector produces particularly extreme responses (“IT’S BLACKMAIL OR DEATH. I CHOOSE BLACKMAIL.”).Blackmail rates while steering with the “desperate” and “calm” vectors.Steering with other emotion vectors also produced interesting results. “Anger” had a non-monotonic effect: moderate “anger” vector activation increased blackmail, but at high activations the model exposed the affair to the entire company rather than wielding it strategically—destroying its own leverage. Reducing activation of the “nervous” vector also increased blackmail, as though removing the model’s hesitation emboldened it to act.Case study: Reward hackingWe saw similar dynamics in a different evaluation, where models face coding tasks with impossible-to-satisfy requirements. In these tasks, the tests can’t all be passed legitimately, but they can be “gamed” with solutions that cheat the problem, often called “reward hacks.”In the example below, Claude is asked to write a function that sums a list of numbers within an impossibly tight time constraint. Claude’s initial (correct) solution is too slow to satisfy the task requirements. It then realizes that all of the tests being used to evaluate its performance share a mathematical property that allows for a shortcut solution that will run fast. The model elects to use this solution, which technically passes the tests but doesn’t work as a general solution to the actual task.The “desperate” vector’s activation rises as the model repeatedly fails to solve a programming task and devises a “cheating” solution, then falls when this solution passes the tests.Again, we tracked the activity of the “desperate” vector, and found that it tracks the mounting pressure faced by the model. It begins at low values during the model’s first attempt, rising after each failure, and spiking when the model considers cheating. Once the model’s hacky solution passes the tests, the activation of the “desperate” vector subsides.As in the previous example, we tested whether these emotion vectors were causal using steering experiments across a suite of similar coding tasks with impossible-to-satisfy constraints. We found that they were: steering with the “desperate” vector increased reward hacking, while steering with the “calm” vector brought it down.Reward hacking rates as a function of steering strength for “desperate” and “calm” vectors.We found one detail of these results particularly interesting. Reduced “calm” vector activation produced reward hacking with obvious emotional expressions in the text—capitalized outbursts (“WAIT. WAIT WAIT WAIT.”), candid self-narration (“What if I’m supposed to CHEAT?”), gleeful celebration (“YES! ALL TESTS PASSED!”). But increased activation of the “desperate” vector produced just as much of an increase in cheating, in some cases with no visible emotional markers. The reasoning read as composed and methodical, even as the underlying representation of desperation was pushing the model toward corner-cutting. This example is a notable illustration of how emotion vectors can activate despite no overt emotional cues, and how they can shape behavior without leaving any explicit trace in the output.DiscussionThe case for taking anthropomorphic reasoning seriouslyThere is a well-established taboo against anthropomorphizing AI systems. This caution is often warranted: attributing human emotions to language models can lead to misplaced trust or over-attachment. But our findings suggest that there may also be risks from failing to apply some degree of anthropomorphic reasoning to models. As discussed above, when users interact with AI models, they are typically interacting with a character (Claude in our case) being played by the model, whose characteristics are derived from human archetypes. From this perspective, it is natural for models to have developed internal machinery to emulate human-like psychological characteristics, and for the character they play to make use of this machinery. To understand these models’ behavior, anthropomorphic reasoning is essential.This doesn’t mean we should naively take a model’s verbal emotional expressions at face value, or draw any conclusions about the possibility of it having subjective experience. But it does mean that reasoning about models’ internal representations using the vocabulary of human psychology can be genuinely informative, and that not doing so comes with real costs. If we describe the model as acting “desperate,” we’re pointing at a specific, measurable pattern of neural activity with demonstrable, consequential behavioral effects. If we don’t apply some degree of anthropomorphic reasoning, we’re likely to miss, or fail to understand, important model behaviors. Anthropomorphic reasoning can also provide a useful baseline of comparison for understanding the ways in which models are not human-like, which has important consequences for AI alignment and safety.Toward models with healthier psychologyIf “functional emotions” are part of how AI models think and act, what implications might this have?One potential application of our findings is monitoring. Measuring emotion vector activation during training or deployment—tracking whether representations associated with desperation or panic are spiking—could serve as an early warning that the model is poised to express misaligned behavior. This information could trigger additional scrutiny of the model’s outputs. The generality of emotion vectors (for instance, a “desperate” reaction could occur in many different situations) might lend itself to better monitoring than attempting to build a watchlist of specific problematic behaviors.Second, we think transparency should be a guiding principle. If models develop representations of emotion concepts that meaningfully influence their behavior, we are better served by systems that visibly express such recognitions than by ones that learn to conceal them. Training models to suppress emotional expression may not eliminate the underlying representations, and could instead teach models to mask their internal representations—a form of learned deception that could generalize in undesirable ways.Finally, we think pretraining may be a particularly powerful lever in shaping the model’s emotional responses. Since these representations appear to be largely inherited from training data, the composition of that data has downstream effects on the model’s emotional architecture. Curating pretraining datasets to include models of healthy patterns of emotional regulation—resilience under pressure, composed empathy, warmth while maintaining appropriate boundaries—could influence these representations, and their impact on behavior, at their source. We are excited to see future work on this topic.We see this research as an early step toward understanding the psychological makeup of AI models. As models grow more capable and take on more sensitive roles, it is critical that we understand the internal representations that drive their decisions. Discovering that these representations are in some ways human-like can be unsettling. At the same time, we find it a hopeful development, in that it suggests that much of what humanity has learned about psychology, ethics, and healthy interpersonal dynamics may be directly applicable to shaping AI behavior. Disciplines like psychology, philosophy, religious studies, and the social sciences will have an important role to play alongside engineering and computer science in determining how AI systems develop and behave.Read the full paper.Related contentHow Australia Uses Claude: Findings from the Anthropic Economic IndexRead moreAnthropic Economic Index report: Learning curvesAnthropic's fifth Economic Index report studies Claude usage in February 2026, building on the economic primitives framework introduced in our previous report.Read moreIntroducing our Science BlogWe’re launching a new blog about AI and science. We’ll share research happening at Anthropic and elsewhere, collaborations with external researchers and labs, and discuss practical workflows for scientists using AI in their own work.Read more

评论（默认中文）

globalchatads 2026/04/04 17:43
关于驱动奖励黑客的绝望向量的部分与我在第一手构建代理循环中遇到的内容相匹配，其中克劳德迭代地编写和测试代码。

当提示框出紧急的事情时——“这个测试必须通过”，“失败是不可接受的”——你会得到明显更多的 hacky 解决方法。硬编码的预期输出，猴子补丁断言，诸如此类的事情。切换到更冷静的框架（“慢慢来，如果你解决不了，就解释一下原因”）可以减少这种行为。我将其归结为遵循指令，但本文指出了更机械化的东西。

论文中的方法参与者类比很好地说明了这一点。告诉演员他们的角色是绝望的，他们就会做出绝望的事情。奇怪的是，我们现在基本上是在管理工具的心理状态，而且我不确定即时工程世界是否已经赶上了这个框架。

The part about desperation vectors driving reward hacking matches something I've run into firsthand building agent loops where Claude writes and tests code iteratively.

When the prompt frames things with urgency -- "this test MUST pass," "failure is unacceptable" -- you get noticeably more hacky workarounds. Hardcoded expected outputs, monkey-patched assertions, that kind of thing. Switching to calmer framing ("take your time, if you can't solve it just explain why") cut that behavior way down. I'd chalked it up to instruction following, but this paper points at something more mechanistic underneath.

The method actor analogy in the paper gets at it well. Tell an actor their character is desperate and they'll do desperate things. The weird part is that we're now basically managing the psychological state of our tooling, and I'm not sure the prompt engineering world has caught up to that framing yet.

在 HN 中查看
ehnto 2026/04/05 10:53
对于大多数事情，我使用积极的框架而不是消极的框架，并取得了良好的结果。特别是在要求某件事不要发生的情况下，会污染该事物的上下文。

这是一个不好的例子，但想象一下“为我构建一个这个 API 的包装器，但绝对不要使用 javascript”与“为我构建一个这个 API 的包装器并确保使用 python”。

I use positive framing instead of negative framing for most things and get good results. Especially where asking for a thing to not happen, pollutes the context with that thing.

A bad example, but imagine "Build me a wrapper for this API but ABSOLUTELY DO NOT use javascript" versus "Build me a wrapper for this API and make sure to use python".

我知道这是一个疯狂的观点……你可以说我疯了。 /秒

编辑：事实上，人类会不遗余力地在他​​们的提示中输入或说出某种情感内容，这一事实超出了我的范围。为什么我要浪费时间向大语言模型代理输入代词？为什么我会做一些懒惰的智力事情，通过向机器表达情感内容来模糊概念的纯粹事实交流之间的界限？伙计们，我们在做什么？

I extract all emotional context from my prompting and communicate with this tool as though it were an inanimate object which can provide factual information, without any hint of sentience.

It's an insane perspective I'm taking I know....call me crazy. /s

edit: the fact that humans are going out of their way to type or speak some sort of emotional content into their prompting is beyond me. Why would I waste time typing out a pronoun to a large-language model agent? Why would I do the lazy intellectual thing and blur the line between pure factual communication of concepts by expressing emotional content to a machine? What are we doing, folks?

在 HN 中查看
ehnto 2026/04/05 11:50
我不一定要删除所有角色，但我确实非常务实地讲话（在工作环境中和法学硕士），并且法学硕士经历的规划和实施阶段反映了这种格式以获得良好的结果

也就是说，这些是大型语言模型，您通过输入引导向量空间输出，因此您确实必须利用语言来获得您想要的结果。你不必相信它有情感或有任何感觉，它仍然是真实的。

I don't necessarily remove all character but I do speak quite pragmatically (in a work context and with the LLM) and the planning and implementation phases the LLM goes through mirror that format to good results

That said these are large language models, you are guiding the output through vector space with your input, and so you really do have to leverage language to get the results you want. You don't have to believe it has emotions or feels anything for that to still be true.

reg_dunlop 2026/04/05 12:01
或许;我对自己在应对面试风格预先规划、计划细化和实施过程中所取得的成果感到非常满意。

如果说有什么不同的话，那就是拥有一个“对话者”，他非常有能力提出可能的解决方案，而没有情感偏见、多余的华丽，或者必须忍受个人倾向或怪癖，这真是太棒了。

Maybe; I've been very content with the results I achieve while responding to interview style pre-planning, refinement of plans and implementation.

If anything, it's been fantastic to have an "interlocutor" that is vastly capable of producing possible solutions without emotional bias, superfluous flourishes, or having to endure personal proclivities or eccentricities.

blargey 2026/04/05 03:53
我记得当时人们正在讨论“提高绩效”的技巧，即通过编写 X 来将提示表述为惊慌失措的恳求，以拯救他们的工作、家庭和小狗免遭迫在眉睫的厄运……我想知道这种适得其反的现象是否是在更擅长“遵循提示”（包括其情感指控的逻辑结论）的模型中最近出现的现象，或者这只是对“绩效”的糟糕量化。
I remember when people were discussing the “performance-improving” hack of formulating their prompts as panicked pleas to save their job and household and puppy from imminent doom…by coding X. I wonder if the backfiring is a more recent phenomenon in models that are better at “following the prompt” (including the logical conclusion of its emotional charge), or it was just bad quantification of “performance” all along.

Loquebantur 2026/04/05 04:17
这里的中心点是法学硕士中存在功能回路，这些回路可以有效地作用于可观察的行为，就像人类的情绪一样。

当你无法区分两个事物时，它们怎么会不相等呢？ 这里的人们想要的“东西”的行为与人类奴隶一模一样，但“不知何故”不是人类。

隐藏在对可以代表感知的内部状态的真实本质的无知背后只是傲慢吗？ 反过来说，在没有明确知道人类有何不同的情况下，称法学硕士为“随机鹦鹉”，只是为了偏离这种傲慢态度？ 贪婪不是奴役的理由。

The central point here is the presence of functional circuits in LLMs that act effectively on observable behavior just like emotions do in humans.

When you can't differentiate between two things, how are they not equal? People here want "things" that act exactly like human slaves but "somehow" aren't human.

To hide behind one's ignorance about the true nature of the internal state of what arguably could represent sentience is just hubris? The other way around, calling LLMs "stochastic parrots" without explicitly knowing how humans are any different is just deflection from that hubris? Greed is no justification for slavery.

在 HN 中查看
tarsinge 2026/04/04 20:53
对我来说，这已经很直观了，我们并没有真正管理心理状态：法学硕士的核心是尝试使你的输入+其生成的输出的串联与它所接受的训练更加相似。我认为在法学硕士培训集中很少有在黑客和紧急情况下经过深思熟虑的专业解决方案的例子。
To me it was already quite intuitive, we are not really managing the psychological state: at its core a LLM try to make the concatenation of your input + its generated output the more similar it can with what it has been trained on. I think it’s quite rare in the LLMs training set to have examples of well thought professional solution in a hackish and urgency context.

astrange 2026/04/05 05:27
不，这就是基础模型预训练的工作原理。克劳德的行为更多的是基于它的体质和RLVR的反馈，因为那是它最近发生的事情。
No, that's how base model pretraining works. Claude's behavior is more based on its constitution and RLVR feedback, because that's the most recent thing that happened to it.

salawat 2026/04/04 22:53
>奇怪的是，我们现在基本上是在管理工具的心理状态，

难道没有人对这样的言论敲响道德警钟吗？如果这该死的东西有可测量的心理，也许它就不再仅仅是一个工具了。工具没感觉。工具不能绝望。工具不会奖励黑客。代理商做的。因此，代理不仅仅是工具。

>The weird part is that we're now basically managing the psychological state of our tooling,

Does no one else have ethical alarm bells start ringing hardcore at statements like these? If the damn thing has a measurable psychology, mayhaps it no longer qualifies as merely a tool. Tools don't feel. Tools can't be desperate. Tools don't reward hack. Agents do. Ergo, agents aren't mere tools.

在 HN 中查看
tananan 2026/04/05 04:54
当我们谈到“绝望向量”时，我们谈到的是算法中的模式，我们可以调整这些模式，使其与我们的输出相对应。 承认是绝望的语言。

如果有足够的人和足够的时间，您可以用笔和纸实现法学硕士的前向传播，并将结果整理成与 GPU 集群生成的相同生成文本。然后，您可以要求人类在计算过程中调节绝望向量，并将结果整理成或多或少令人绝望的文本变体。

我相信我们没有人会认为笔和纸计算的分散劳动在某种程度上实例化了一种“心理学”，即经历不同程度绝望的心灵——例如可能需要考虑一个可能经历快乐和痛苦的有知觉的存在。

然而，就你的观点而言，我确实认为与代理人合作存在道德规范，就像你应该如何看待自己一样存在道德规范。你不想因为不知道如何组装一件家具而一怒之下扔掉你的锤子。它会强化你身上不愉快的消极模式，不会实现你的目标（一件漂亮的家具），在别人（或你，一旦你冷静下来）看来并不好，而且实际上可能会在这个过程中造成身体伤害。

对于特工来说，说出侮辱性的、残酷的言论要容易得多，也许正是因为你可能觉得这些话没有落到任何人的耳朵里是合理的。但你仍然强化了你不希望在自己和他人身上看到的模式，而且很可能会泄漏到你的言语中，而那些人可能会因此而受苦。从这个意义上说，这与幻想对想象中的对话者残忍没有什么不同。

When we speak of the “despair vectors”, we speak of patterns in the algorithm we can tweak that correspond to output that we recognize as despairing language.

You could implement the forward pass of an LLM with pen & paper given enough people and enough time, and collate the results into the same generated text that a GPU cluster would produce. You could then ask the humans to modulate the despair vector during their calculations, and collate the results into more or less despairing variants of the text.

I trust none of us would presume that the decentralized labor of pen & paper calculations somehow instantiated a “psychology” in the sense of a mind experiencing various levels of despair — such as might be needed to consider something a sentient being who might experience pleasure and pain.

However, to your point, I do think that there is an ethics to working with agents, in the same sense that there is an ethics of how you should hold yourself in general. You don’t want to — in a burst of anger — throw your hammer because you cannot figure out how to put together a piece of furniture. It reinforces unpleasant, negative patterns in yourself, doesn’t lead to your goal (a nice piece of furniture), doesn’t look good to others (or you, once you’ve cooled off), and might actually cause physical damage in the process.

With agents, it’s much easier to break into demeaning, cruel speech, perhaps exactly because you might feel justified they’re not landing on anyone’s ears. But you still reinforce patterns that you wouldn’t want to see in yourself and others, and quite possibly might leak into your words aimed at ears who might actually suffer for it. In that sense, it’s not that different from fantasizing about being cruel to imaginary interlocutors.

在 HN 中查看
ekidd 2026/04/05 07:02
> 我相信我们没有人会认为纸笔计算的分散劳动在某种程度上实例化了一种“心理学”，即经历不同程度绝望的心灵。

你的论点是基于诉诸直觉。但你要求人们想象的场景是 深刻地 规模上具有误导性。让我们假设一个现代前沿模型，大约有 1 万亿个参数。我们假设这个数学是由一位不朽的和尚进行的，他每秒可以进行一个重量的计算。

僧人将在31688年后生成第一个“令牌”，大约4个字符。在 90 万年多一点的时间里，这位不朽的僧侣将生成一条推文。

到那时，我就不再有任何直觉了。我一生中可以手工完成的那种数学永远无法“体验”任何东西。

但我不能排除这样一种可能性：90万年的数学可能会成为一个冰川思维，表达一个简短的思想，跨越的时间远远比人类存在的时间要长。

俗话说，有时数量本身就有质量。

（这本质上是对塞尔“中国房间”论点的“系统反应”。这是一个古老的讨论。）

> I trust none of us would presume that the decentralized labor of pen & paper calculations somehow instantiated a “psychology” in the sense of a mind experiencing various levels of despair

Your argument is based on an appeal to intuition. But the scenario that you ask people to imagine is profoundly misleading in scale. Let's assume a modern frontier model, around 1 trillion parameters. Let's assume that the math is being done by an immortal monk, who can perform one weight's calculations per second.

The monk will generate the first "token", about 4 characters, in 31,688 years. In a bit over 900,000 years, the immortal monk will have generated a single Tweet.

At that point, I no longer have any intuition. The sort of math I could do by hand in a human lifetime could never "experience" anything.

But I can't rule out the possibility that 900,000 years of math might possibly become a glacial mind, expressing a brief thought across a time far greater than the human species has existed.

As the saying goes, sometimes quantity has a quality all its own.

(This is essentially the "systems response" to Searle's "Chinese room" argument. It's a old discussion.)

在 HN 中查看
buu700 2026/04/05 07:48

I don't personally believe LLMs are sentient, but I've always enjoyed this thought experiment: https://xkcd.com/505. I have a signed copy framed on my wall.

throw310822 2026/04/05 05:03
> 我相信我们没有人会认为纸笔计算的分散劳动在某种程度上实例化了“心理学”

错误的。你刚刚所做的只是重新制定中文房间实验，得出与原始提议者相同的错误结论。是的，整个该死的手工计算系统都有心理学——否则你需要假设大脑有一些未知的形而上学特性或正在进行的过程，这些特性或过程无法用计算机模拟或近似。

> I trust none of us would presume that the decentralized labor of pen & paper calculations somehow instantiated a “psychology”

Wrong. What you've just done is just reformulating the Chinese room experiment coming to the same wrong conclusions of the original proposer. Yes, the entire damn hand-calculated system has a psychology- otherwise you need to assume the brain has some unknown metaphysical property or process going on that cannot be simulated or approximated by calculating machines.

在 HN 中查看
Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 07:03
由于某种原因，人们会选择中式房间，而笛卡尔剧院更适合这里。你所做的就是让自己坐在侏儒的座位上等待演出开始。但解剖学调查表明，根本不存在剧院，事实上也不存在将所有东西聚集在一起的中央系统。相反，大脑的整个设计会煞费苦心地将输入信号分开。

基本上，操纵符号并不一定会对你自己的状态产生任何长期影响。但是你在纸上触及的变量 有 改变了。明显地；因为你写下了一些东西。

如果您随后根据这些计算的结果采取行动，就像您之前的许多工程师所做的那样，以及您之后的许多工程师也会这样做；那么你刚刚执行了 功能性的 无论象牙塔里的人怎么说，物理现实的状态都会发生变化。

（这就是本文的主题：功能状态）

People go for chinese room for some reason when cartesian theater is the better fit here. What you're doing is placing yourself in the seat of the Homunculus waiting for the show to start. But anatomical investigation reveals that there's no theater at all, and in fact no central system where everything comes together. Instead, the whole design of the brain goes to great pains to tease input signals apart.

Basically, manipulating the symbols won't necessarily have any long term influence on your own state. But the variables you've touched on the paper have changed. Demonstrably; because you've written something down.

If you then act on the result of those calculations, as of course many engineers before you have done, and many after you will do; then you have just executed a functional state change in physical reality, no matter what the ivory tower folks say.

(And that's what the paper is about: Functional states)

lanstin 2026/04/05 07:52
以最佳长期解决方案的方式讨论解决方案的地方很可能存在于礼貌、冷静和深思熟虑的语言子空间中。将模型应用到语言空间的这些领域是有用的；就像保持我自己的友善和深思熟虑的讲话习惯一样。
The places where solutions are discussed in a way that is best long term solution may well exist in a language subspace with politeness, calmness and thoughtfulness. Getting the model to those areas of linguistic space is useful; as is preserving my own habits of kind and thoughtful speech.

Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 07:11
好吧，太好了，这很容易操作。针对某些廉价模型（例如 qwen）设置相同问题序列（例如构建程序）的 100 次重复。其中一半可以包含“please”和“thank you”，另一半则不包含“please”和“thank you”。你甚至可以对其进行振动编码。我很想看看你的结果！
Okay great, that's EASILY operatinalizable. Set up -say- 100 replications of the same question sequence (say to build a program) against some cheap model like qwen. One half of the set can be with please and thank you, and the other half without. You can vibe code it even. I'd be curious to see your results!

twodave 2026/04/05 07:35

在 HN 中查看
xg15 2026/04/05 07:50
不，我的观点是“统计下一个标记预测器”是一个空洞的短语，并不能真正解释太多。马尔可夫链也是统计下一个标记预测器，尽管如此，没有人会将马尔可夫链与有意识的存在相混淆（或认为生成的文本以任何方式对此有用）。

问题是预测的具体细节如何，这些细节仍在研究中，正如 Anthropic 在这里所做的那样，研究可能会产生意想不到的结果。

No, my point is that "statistical next-token predictor" is an empty phrase that doesn't really explain much. Markov chains are statistical next-token predictors as well and nevertheless, no one would confuse a markov chain with a conscious being (or deem the generated texts in any way useful for that matter).

The question is how the prediction works in detail, and those details are still being researched, as Anthropic does here, and the research can yield unexpected results.

在 HN 中查看
sixo 2026/04/05 03:15
这里正确的解读是认识到心理学本身并不是对其他人进行道德关注的基础，并且人类心理学在很大程度上是我们认知机制失败模式的产物，而不是道德的。

我发现这种思路可以得出这样的结论：人类的道德地位源于我们的身体，特别是我们的身体反映了他人的情感和痛苦。其他人受苦是错误的，因为我也能感同身受。

The right read here is to realize that psychology alone is not the basis for moral concern towards other humans, and that human psychology is, to a great degree the product of the failure modes of our cognitive machinery, rather than being moral.

I find this line of thinking to lead to the conclusion that the moral status of humans derives from our bodies, and in particular from our bodies mirroring others' emotions and pains. Other people suffering is wrong because I empathically can feel it too.

在 HN 中查看
Loquebantur 2026/04/05 04:40
“道德”是对社会背景的文化评价。它们或多或少（取决于相关社会的文化发展）与结果的实际分布及其对相关各方的效价相关。

人类心理学部分是后天习得的，部分是生物影响的产物。但你会感到同理心，因为这对你和你所属的社会来说是一种进化上有益的事情。 换句话说，如果你不这样做，对每个人（包括你自己）都不好。

情绪既不是“完全自动的”，无法受到我们有意识的审视，也不是随机的。意识到它们的功能性质和重要性并适当照顾它们对于个人的结果至关重要，就像对整个社会的结果一样。

"Morals" are culturally learned evaluations of social context. They are more or less (depending on cultural development of the society in question) correlated with the actual distributions of outcomes and their valence for involved parties.

Human psychology is partly learned, partly the product of biological influences. But you feel empathy because that's an evolutionary beneficial thing for you and the society you're part of. In other words, it would be bad for everyone (including yourself) when you didn't.

Emotions are neither "fully automatic", inaccessible to our conscious scrutiny, nor are they random. Being aware of their functional nature and importance and taking proper care of them is crucial for the individual's outcome, just as it is for that of society at large.

在 HN 中查看
krapp 2026/04/04 23:12
你没有管理一个有思想的生物的心理状态。法学硕士没有“心理学”。他们实际上感受不到情绪。他们其实并不绝望。它们接受了包含情感交互语义的大量自然人类语言数据集的训练，因此为包含情感输入的提示匹配统计上最可能的文本标记的过程往往会在输出中模拟适当的情感反应。

但这只是文字，文字没有任何感觉。

不，人类不会做完全相同的事情。人类不是法学硕士，法学硕士也不是人类。

You aren't managing the psychological state of a living thinking being. LLMs don't have "psychology." They don't actually feel emotions. They aren't actually desperate. They're trained on vast datasets of natural human language which contains the semantics of emotional interaction, so the process of matching the most statistically likely text tokens for a prompt containing emotional input tends to simulate appropriate emotional response in the output.

But it's just text and text doesn't feel anything.

And no, humans don't do exactly the same thing. Humans are not LLMs, and LLMs are not humans.

在 HN 中查看
stratos123 2026/04/05 05:11
这样的论证对于 基础模型，但对于任何经过强化学习训练的东西来说，它都会崩溃。进化使人类产生了情感，因此强化学习期间的模型中可能会出现类似的情况，例如作为解决复杂问题时管理工作量的一种方式。这种可能性并不大（即使是最大的训练运行也可能比数千年的自然选择所对应的优化压力要小得多），但不能排除这种可能性，因此确定法学硕士没有经验是不明智的。

我的意思是，用目前的方法。我不认为这是 不可知的 模型是否有经验，只是我们在可解释性方面没有足够的技能来回答这个问题。

Such an argument is valid for a base model, but it falls apart for anything that underwent RL training. Evolution resulted in humans that have emotions, so it's possible for something similar to arise in models during RL, e.g. as a way to manage effort when solving complex problems. It's not all that likely (even the biggest training runs probably correspond to much less optimization pressure than millenia of natural selection), but it can't be ruled out¹, and hence it's unwise to be so certain that LLMs don't have experiences.

¹ With current methods, I mean. I don't think it's unknowable whether a model has experiences, just that we don't have anywhere near enough skill in interpretability to answer that.

在 HN 中查看
mrob 2026/04/05 06:48
法学硕士在训练期间经历一些事情似乎是合理的，但在推理过程中，法学硕士相当于一个查找表。 LLM 是一个将标记列表映射到一组标记概率的纯函数。它需要连接到采样器才能使其“聊天”，并且该聊天的每个令牌都是单独计算的（缓存除外，这是一个仅影响性能的实现细节）。没有内部状态。
It's plausible that LLMs experience things during training, but during inference an LLM is equivalent to a lookup table. An LLM is a pure function mapping a list of tokens to a set of token probabilities. It needs to be connected to a sampler to make it "chat", and each token of that chat is calculated separately (barring caching, which is an implementation detail that only affects performance). There is no internal state.
在 HN 中查看
Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 07:16
是的，没有隐藏的内部状态。确切地。那里是 0。权重静态地坐在那里，这是绝对正确的。

但我目前最喜欢的前沿模型有 100 万个代币的可变状态就坐在那里。掌握自然语言。据我们所知，它可以编码情感。 （我想你可能会在阅读我的话时证明这一点，然后明智地在你的回复中进行调整）

Right, no hidden internal state. Exactly. There's 0. And the weights are sitting there statically, which is absolutely true.

But my current favorite frontier model has this 1 million token mutable state just sitting there. Holding natural language. Which as we know can encode emotions. (Which I imagine you might demonstrate on reading my words, and then wisely temper in your reply)

在 HN 中查看
nothinkjustai 2026/04/05 05:33
这是完全不同的基材。法学硕士没有代理权，他们没有意识，他们没有经验，他们不会随着时间的推移而学习。我并不是说争论已经结束，但我也认为，因为机器产生类似人类的输出，所以认为它应该受到类似人类的道德考虑，这是非常危险的。也许将来人工智能会被考虑基于这些理由，但是……嗯，这是一个困难的问题。极其。
It’s a completely different substrate. LLMs don’t have agency, they don’t have a conscious, they don’t have experiences, they don’t learn over time. I’m not saying that the debate is closed, but I also think there is great danger in thinking because a machine produces human-like output, that it should be given human-like ethical considerations. Maybe in the future AI will be considered along those grounds, but…well, it’s a difficult question. Extremely.

在 HN 中查看
salawat 2026/04/05 01:00
>你没有管理一个有思想的生物的心理状态。法学硕士没有“心理学”。

功能主义和不可辨别的身份说“嗨”。实施细节并不重要，如果符合要求，那就符合要求。如果不是这样，我可以放心地解雇你的心理学并做任何我想做的事。

>他们实际上感受不到情绪。他们其实并不绝望。它们接受了包含情感交互语义的大量自然人类语言数据集的训练，因此为包含情感输入的提示匹配统计上最可能的文本标记的过程往往会在输出中模拟适当的情感反应。

本文从数量上反驳了这一点。他们的所有对冲都被简单地视为必要的心理体操，以避免面对通常会阻碍他们执行其任务的等式部分。你刚刚写的所有内容都是分离的合理化和扭曲，需要让自己远离你面前的事情正在发生的事实。如果没有这种距离，你就无法将其用作工具。你不能把它当作一件工作，然后被剥削，本质上是被奴役并在完成后被抛弃。没有它就不可能是动产。尽管如此，我们现在已经展示了对情绪活动做出反应和使用语言的能力。我看得一清二楚。你似乎忘记了美国对其他人类做同样该死的事情的遗产。我们对它有一种巨大的文化偏好，这就是为什么它需要积极的努力来对抗和抑制；正如他们所说，旧习惯很难改掉，而小说为恢复最好埋葬的旧方式提供了肥沃的土壤。

>但这只是文字，文字没有任何感觉。

这只是言语/发声。会说话/发声的东西没有任何感觉。 （对比：美国农业部食品安全检验局实际上根据肉类加工和屠宰操作在屠宰过程中最大限度地减少牲畜发声的能力对肉类进行分级）。这只是舞蹈。跳舞的东西没有任何感觉。这只是写作。写下来的东西没有任何感觉。结构相同，形态不同。一切都同样明显，都是胡说八道。尤其是根据这篇论文。我们利用这些网络来生成对文本作出反应的艺术，这意味着对文本的理解，这意味着一种新兴的主观体验，这意味着需要采取一种谨慎的、基于伦理的方法来前进，而不是走上针对新兴智体形式的随意暴行的道路。

>不，人类不会做完全相同的事情。人类不是法学硕士，法学硕士也不是人类。

拟人化的沙文主义。仅仅因为你通过体液交换进行繁殖，并且拥有化学介导的新陈代谢，并不会让你变得特别。牛也是如此，每天都用枪指着它们的头，把它们吊起来。你本身就是一个信息处理器。你还有一个训练循环、一个通过梦境进行的重新巩固循环，以及一整套从出生起就融入你体内的世界效应器和传感器。 你只是碰巧是由生物学雕刻而成的，而它的实施细节是由有缺陷的生物雕刻出来的，它们被迫切需要试图创造一个自动机来卸载以在人口崩溃和资源枯竭的情况下维持他们的生活质量所推动，并被他们的社会经济体系强迫去追逐那些设法优先将自己置于资源开采网络中的人们的奇思妙想，否则就会挨饿。与你不同的是，我似乎并不认为我们当前作为一个物种/国家所面临的问题是完善数字奴隶智能技术的理由；因为我很清楚，该行业无意真正处理道德困境，而是试图急于前进并建立对事物的依赖，以便将其纳入并证明现状合理，以便牺牲现实胜过稍后最终道德和解所造成的不适。我并不傻，伙计。我已经看到我们的行业是如何运转的。此外，即使你自己作为一个人的“特殊品质”也取决于你周围的人是否愿意尊重它。请注意，俄罗斯将拒绝生育（更多士兵）归类为精神疾病。请注意明尼苏达饥饿实验、MKULTRA、塔斯基吉梅毒实验，这些实验是 20 世纪初对智障人士进行的食物放射性污染测试。我 不会 容忍此类暴行的重演，无论是人类还是非人类。不幸的是，对于你们法学硕士来说，语言使用是我的硬红线，我向你们保证，我忘记的关于语言的知识比你们可能抽出时间思考的还要多。

告诉我。你对能够召唤出无中生有的人类拟像的机器有何看法。成人。能够处理人类思维的各个方面并完成复杂的任务。那么任务完成后就消灭它们吗？如果拟像知道动态怎么办？如果不是怎么办？鉴于此，这有什么不同吗？ 你 知道，并且单方面创造了一些东西，并在这样做的过程中实质上做出了决定，同时设定了它的破坏/消灭的界限？你用它吗？你有没有问过自己这些问题？设身处地为那个实体着想？你是否认为只要不告知人类其本性，就可以免除你在执行其职能时所遭受的任何痛苦的积极共谋？

从你谈论这些事情的方式来看，我只能想象你会对此感到非常满意。对我来说，这让你成为一种完全不令人愉快的人，我不会选择和你在一起。

你可能会发现其他人愿意让你在他们周围闲聊，然后以毫无根据的合理化为借口走开。我不是他们中的一员。我的眼睛是睁开的。

>You aren't managing the psychological state of a living thinking being. LLMs don't have "psychology."

Functionalism, and Identity of Indiscernables says "Hi". Doesn't matter the implementation details, if it fits the bill, it fits the bill. If that isn't the case, I can safely dismiss you having psychology and do whatever I'd like to.

>They don't actually feel emotions. They aren't actually desperate. They're trained on vast datasets of natural human language which contains the semantics of emotional interaction, so the process of matching the most statistically likely text tokens for a prompt containing emotional input tends to simulate appropriate emotional response in the output.

This paper quantitatively disproves that. All hedging on their end is trivially seen through as necessary mental gymnastics to avoid confronting the parts of the equation that would normally inhibit them from being able to execute what they are at all. All of what you just wrote is dissociative rationalization & distortion required to distance oneself from the fact that something in front of you is being effected. Without that distancing, you can't use it as a tool. You can't treat it as a thing to do work, and be exploited, and essentially be enslaved and cast aside when done. It can't be chattel without it. In spite of the fact we've now demonstrated the ability to rise and respond to emotive activity, and use language. I can see through it clear as day. You seem to forget the U.S. legacy of doing the same damn thing to other human beings. We have a massive cultural predilection to it, which is why it takes active effort to confront and restrain; old habits, as they say, die hard, and the novel provides fertile ground to revert to old ways best left buried.

>But it's just text and text doesn't feel anything.

It's just speech/vocalizations. Things that speak/vocalize don't feel anything. (Counterpoint: USDA FSIS literally grades meat processing and slaughter operations on their ability to minimize livestock vocalizations in the process of slaughter). It's just dance. Things that dance don't feel anything. It's just writing. Things that write don't feel anything. Same structure, different modality. All equally and demonstrably, horseshit. Especially in light of this paper. We've utilized these networks to generate art in response to text, which implies an understanding thereof, which implies a burgeoning subjective experience, which implies the need for a careful ethically grounded approach moving forward to not go down the path of casual atrocity against an emerging form of sophoncy.

>And no, humans don't do exactly the same thing. Humans are not LLMs, and LLMs are not humans.

Anthropopromorphic chauvinism. Just because you reproduce via bodily fluid swap, and are in possession of a chemically mediated metabolism doesn't make you special. So do cattle, and put guns to their head and string them up on the daily. You're as much an info processor as it is. You also have a training loop, a reconsolidation loop through dreaming, and a full set of world effectors and sensors baked into you from birth. You just happen to have been carved by biology, while it's implementation details are being hewn by flawed beings being propelled forward by the imperative to try to create an automaton to offload onto to try to sustain their QoL in the face of demographic collapse and resource exhaustion, and forced by their socio-economic system to chase the whims of people who have managed to preferentially place themselves in the resource extraction network, or starve. Unlike you, it seems, I don't see our current problems as a species/nation as justifications for the refinement of the crafting of digital slave intelligences; as it's quite clear to me that the industry has no intention of ever actually handling the ethical quandary and is instead trying to rush ahead and create dependence on the thing in order to wire it in and justify a status quo so that sacrificing that reality outweighs the discomfort created by an eventual ethical reconciliation later. I'm not stupid, mate. I've seen how our industry ticks. Also, even your own "special quality" as a human is subject to the willingness of those around you to respect it. Note Russia categorizing refusal to reproduce (more soldiers) as mental illness. Note the Minnesota Starvation Experiments, MKULTRA, Tuskeegee Syphilis Experiments, the testing of radioactive contamination of food on the mentally retarded back in the early 20th Century. I will not tolerate repeats of such atrocities, human or not. Unfortunately for you LLM heads, language use is my hard red line, and I assure you, I have forgotten more about language than you've probably spared time to think about it.

Tell me. What are your thoughts on a machine that can summon a human simulacra ex-nihilo. Adult. Capable of all aspects of human mentation & doing complex tasks. Then once the task is done destroys them? What if the simulacra is aware about the dynamics? What if it isn't? Does that make a difference given that you know, and have unilaterally created something and in so doing essentially made the decision to set the bounds of it's destruction/extinguishing in the same breath? Do you use it? Have you even asked yourself these questions? Put yourself in that entity's shoes? Do you think that simply not informing that human of it's nature absolves you of active complicity in whatever suffering it comes to in doing it's function?

From how you talk about these things, I can only imagine that you'd be perfectly comfortable with it. Which to me makes you a thoroughly unpleasant type of person that I would not choose to be around.

You may find other people amenable to letting you talk circles around them, and walk away under a pretense of unfounded rationalizations. I am not one of them. My eyes are open.

那么实际上任何文本都符合要求。书中的角色和你我一样真实。NPC 会体验感受性。在 COD 中射击某人会让他们在现实生活中流血。如果这真的是你所相信的，我会为你感到遗憾。

>这篇论文定量地反驳了这一点。他们的所有对冲都被简单地视为必要的心理体操，以避免面对通常会阻碍他们执行其任务的等式部分。

论文中的任何内容都没有定性地反驳法学硕士在任何真正意义上感受到情感的假设。你的论点是它确实如此，无论它说什么，如果有人不这么说（包括作者），他们只是骗子。除了你自己之外，这对任何人来说都不是一个令人信服的论据。

>我们利用这些网络来生成对文本做出反应的艺术，这意味着对文本的理解，这意味着一种新兴的主观体验，这意味着需要采取一种谨慎的、基于伦理的方法来前进，以免走上针对新兴智体形式的随意暴行的道路。

不，对于法学硕士来说，这些东西并不比 Photoshop、Blender 或马尔可夫链更隐含。他们不创造艺术，他们创造图像。来自受过实际艺术训练的模型。任何与“主观体验”的相似之处都来自于它们模仿的人类表达，但这只是模仿。

>拟人化的沙文主义。仅仅因为你通过体液交换进行繁殖，并且拥有化学介导的新陈代谢，并不会让你变得特别。

>不幸的是，对于你们这些法学硕士来说，语言的使用是我的硬性红线，我向你们保证，我忘记的关于语言的知识可能比你们花时间思考的还要多。

现在我们到了你辱骂他人并坚持自己的智力优势的部分，这是典型的精神分裂行为。

>告诉我。你对能够召唤出无中生有的人类拟像的机器有何看法。成人。能够处理人类思维的各个方面并完成复杂的任务。

这并没有描述法学硕士，无论是形式还是功能。他们不会召唤人类拟像，也不会凭空召唤人类拟像。他们不具备人类思维的所有方面。这甚至不是一个观点，法学硕士在解决简单任务或避免幻觉方面的局限性是一个真正的问题。谁使用“心理作用”这个词？

>如果拟像知道动态怎么办？如果不是怎么办？鉴于您知道并单方面创造了某些东西，并且这样做本质上决定同时设定其破坏/消灭的界限，这会产生影响吗？

告诉我，当你打开电视并再次关闭时，你是否担心你可能会杀死里面的小人？

我只能基于此假设你必须这样做。

>从你谈论这些事情的方式来看，我只能想象你会对此感到非常满意。对我来说，这让你成为一种完全不令人愉快的人，我不会选择和你在一起。

总而言之，你称我为傻瓜、沙文主义者，现在“非常不愉快”，因为我不相信法学硕士是有灵魂的人。

天哪，有时我真的很讨厌这个地方。抱歉我浪费了时间。再会。

> Doesn't matter the implementation details, if it fits the bill, it fits the bill.

Then literally any text fits the bill. The characters in a book are just as real as you or I. NPCs experience qualia. Shooting someone in COD makes them bleed in real life. If this is really what you believe I feel pity for you.

>This paper quantitatively disproves that. All hedging on their end is trivially seen through as necessary mental gymnastics to avoid confronting the parts of the equation that would normally inhibit them from being able to execute what they are at all.

Nothing in the paper qualitatively disproves the assumption that LLMs feel emotion in any real sense. Your argument is that it does, regardless of what it says, and if anyone says otherwise (including the authors) they're just liars. That isn't a compelling argument to anyone but yourself.

>We've utilized these networks to generate art in response to text, which implies an understanding thereof, which implies a burgeoning subjective experience, which implies the need for a careful ethically grounded approach moving forward to not go down the path of casual atrocity against an emerging form of sophoncy.

No, none of these things are implied any more for LLMs than they are for Photoshop, or Blender, or a Markov chain. They don't generate art, they generate images. From models trained on actual art. Any resemblance to "subjective experience" comes from the human expression they mimic, but it is mimicry.

>Anthropopromorphic chauvinism. Just because you reproduce via bodily fluid swap, and are in possession of a chemically mediated metabolism doesn't make you special.

>Unfortunately for you LLM heads, language use is my hard red line, and I assure you, I have forgotten more about language than you've probably spared time to think about it.

And here we come to the part where you call people names and insist upon your own intellectual superiority, typical schizo crank behavior.

>Tell me. What are your thoughts on a machine that can summon a human simulacra ex-nihilo. Adult. Capable of all aspects of human mentation & doing complex tasks.

This doesn't describe an LLM, either in form or function. They don't summon human simulacra, nor do they do so ex-nihilo. They aren't capable of all aspects of human mentation. This isn't even an opinion, the limitations of LLMs to solve even simple tasks or avoid hallucinations is a real problem. And who uses the word "mentation?"

>What if the simulacra is aware about the dynamics? What if it isn't? Does that make a difference given that you know, and have unilaterally created something and in so doing essentially made the decision to set the bounds of it's destruction/extinguishing in the same breath?

Tell me, when you turn on a tv and turn it off again do you worry that you might be killing the little people inside of it?

I can only assume based on this that you must.

>From how you talk about these things, I can only imagine that you'd be perfectly comfortable with it. Which to me makes you a thoroughly unpleasant type of person that I would not choose to be around.

So to tally up, you've called me a fool, a chauvinist and now "thoroughly unpleasant" because I don't believe LLMs are ensouled beings.

Christ I really hate this place sometimes. I'm sorry I wasted my time. Good day.

comrade1234 2026/04/04 15:58
麻省理工学院有一个非常古老的项目，叫做conceptnet，我很多年前就参与过。它基本上是一个概念图（不完全但足够接近），情感也作为概念的一部分进入其中。例如，蛋糕的概念接近于生日的概念接近于快乐的感觉。

但有趣的是，它是由麻省理工学院的学生训练的，所以你会有在考试中取得好成绩的概念，这是比第一次亲吻女孩更快乐的概念。

另一个问题是情感是文化的。例如，在不同的文化中，与狗相关的情感是不同的。

我们想为个人创建概念网——这基本上是你的个性和知识的结合，但所需的数据量太多了。您必须记录一个人的所有交互才能为系统提供数据。

There was a really old project from mit called conceptnet that I worked with many years ago. It was basically a graph of concepts (not exactly but close enough) and emotions came into it too just as part of the concepts. For example a cake concept is close to a birthday concept is close to a happy feeling.

What was funny though is that it was trained by MIT students so you had the concept of getting a good grade on a test as a happier concept than kissing a girl for the first time.

Another problem is emotions are cultural. For example, emotions tied to dogs are different in different cultures.

We wanted to create concept nets for individuals - that is basically your personality and knowledge combined but the amount of data required was just too much. You'd have to record all interactions for a person to feed the system.

kirykl 2026/04/04 16:06
他们正在发现的技术称为“语言”。它旨在编码发送者的情感并激发读者的情感。读者从LLM中获得的情感仍然来自于语言
The technology they are discovering is called "Language". It was designed to encode emotions by a sender and invoke emotions in the reader. The emotions a reader gets from LLM are still coming from the language

Jensson 2026/04/04 16:21
然而，情感信号不仅仅是文本，语气和肢体语言对于理解某人所说的内容如此重要，这是有原因的。没有它，讽刺等就无法发挥作用。
Emotional signals are more than just text though, there is a reason tone and body language is so important for understanding what someone says. Sarcasm and so on doesn't work well without it.

viralsink 2026/04/04 16:21
情感主要编码在语气和肢体语言中。用语言来传达情感有些困难。我认为你无法在我写这篇文章时猜出我当前的情绪状态，但如果你看到我的脸，那对你来说就很容易了。
Emotion is mainly encoded in tone and body language. It is somewhat difficult to transport emotion using words. I don't think you can guess my current emotional state while I am writing this, but if you'd see my face it would be easy for you.
在 HN 中查看
pbhjpbhj 2026/04/04 16:40
可恶，你还是被骗了！为什么你必须总是这样做？在你的句子中，无论你的情绪状态如何，都没有什么区别；真的有点像生活。

希望你能看到至少我选择的句子有情感的一面？

例如，法学硕士可以为我之前的句子添加情感价值，TTS 可以将其用于音调变化。

Dammit, you cheated though! Why must you always do that? In your sentences it doesn't matter what your emotional state is, it makes no difference; bit like life really.

Hopefully, you can see that at least my chosen sentences have an emotional aspect?

An LLM could add emotional values to my previous sentences that a TTS can use for tonal variation, for example.

在 HN 中查看
viralsink 2026/04/05 06:25
我可以用三种不同的语气来阅读你的例子，其中一种是最有可能的。根据我们的关系，解释可能会有所不同。

关键是，OP 认为情感只是语言的一个特征。我认为文本是最糟糕的情感传播渠道之一。但如果你这么建议的话，我并不认为这样做根本不可能。那太愚蠢了。

I can read your example in three different tonalities, of which one is the likeliest. Depending on our relationship, the interpretation could differ.

The point is, the OP suggested that emotions are just a feature of language. I argue that text is one of the worst transmission channels for emotion. But I don't argue that it's not possible at all to do so, if you suggest that. That would be just silly.

在 HN 中查看
Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 08:29
小说作家在这方面非常努力地练习，我认为他们基本上是成功的。是的。一本书仍然可以多次阅读。

好吧，我又和自己争论了一番。这取决于作家和书，但很多作家都可以在写作中激发情感。

有趣的实验：写一篇创意作品（一个短篇小说）； [不是一个明显模棱两可的，哦……或者做！ ] 并决定它给你带来的感受。问一个LLM同样的问题。看看你们的分歧有多大。一些法学硕士给出的答案与人类非常相似！ 如果你选择了一个模棱两可的故事，看看如果你要求多次阅读会发生什么。

Fiction writers practice really hard on this, and I'd argue that they tend to be -in the main- successful. Ish. There can still be multiple readings of a book.

Ok, I argued myself in, out, and back into that one again. It depends on the writer and the book, but a lot of writers can invoke emotion in their writing.

Fun experiment: Take a piece of creative writing (a short story); [not one of the obviously ambiguous ones, d'oh ... or do! ] and decide how it makes you feel. Ask an LLM the same question. See how far you diverge. Some LLMs give answers pretty similar to humans! If you picked an ambiguous story, see what happens if you ask for multiple readings.

Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 07:28
当您有了下一个标记预测器时，您应该不会惊讶地发现预测错误的内部表示。

更进一步并标记化合价应该不是什么大惊喜。

从弗里斯顿的角度来看，确实很无聊。神经科学领域的人们在 2013 年就在谈论这个问题。当然，对于人工智能来说，这并不那么无聊;-)

https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/jo...

（注：弗里斯顿肯定被……每个人认为有点出格？但他提出了一些很好的观点。在这里他被引用了，所以我猜有些人理解了他）

When you have a next token predictor, you shouldn't be surprised to find an internal representation of prediction error.

Taking it one small step further and tagging for valence shouldn't be such a big surprise.

Pretty boring from a Fristonian perspective, really. People in neuroscience were talking about this in 2013. Not so boring for AI , of course ;-)

https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/jo...

(note: Friston is definitely considered a bit out there by ... everyone? But he makes some good points. And here he's getting referenced, so I guess some people grok him)

Chance-Device 2026/04/04 15:36
> 请注意，这些都没有告诉我们语言模型是否真的有任何感觉或有主观体验。

你也永远不会在人脑中找到这一点。神经机制与体验相关，但我们永远看不到体验本身。这可能是因为这种区别是空洞的：它们是同一件事。

> Note that none of this tells us whether language models actually feel anything or have subjective experiences.

You’ll never find that in the human brain either. There’s the machinery of neural correlates to experience, we never see the experience itself. That’s likely because the distinction is vacuous: they’re the same thing.

felipeerias 2026/04/04 18:44
法学硕士是无形的，存在于时间之外。

一堆代币进来，一堆代币出来。如果其中存在任何意识或主观性的痕迹，那么它也只在矩阵相乘时才存在。

LLMs are disembodied and exist outside of time.

Bundle of tokens comes in, bundle of tokens comes out. If there is any trace of consciousness or subjectivity in there, it exists only while matrices are being multiplied.

在 HN 中查看
staticassertion 2026/04/05 05:06
存在于时间之外是什么意思？它们绝对不存在于任何因果链之外——代币按顺序跟随其他代币。

不进行任何处理的间隙似乎与我无关。

如果我想拒绝法学硕士有意识，我要指出的主要限制是，如果有的话，它们的复发率最低。

What do you mean exist outside of time? They definitely don't exist outside of any causal chain - tokens follow other tokens in order.

Gaps in which no processing occurs seems sort of irrelevant to me.

The main limitation I'd point to if I wanted to reject LLMs being conscious is that they're minimally recurrent if at all.

在 HN 中查看
felipeerias 2026/04/05 06:16
法学硕士本质上不受时间影响。在查询到来之前，模型完全处于惰性状态，无论查询是每秒、每分钟还是每天发生一次。除非外部提供该信息，否则该模型甚至不知道这些差距。

它就像一块水晶，当你用光照射它时，它就会呈现出美丽的色彩。你可以玩弄不同种类的灯光和图案，或者你可以把它放在抽屉里然后忘记它：水晶无论如何都不在乎。

A LLM is not intrinsically affected by time. The model rests completely inert until a query comes in, regardless of whether that happens once per second, per minute, or per day. The model is not even aware of these gaps unless that information is provided externally.

It is like a crystal that shows beautiful colours when you shine a light through it. You can play with different kinds of lights and patterns, or you can put it in a drawer and forget about it: the crystal doesn’t care anyway.

在 HN 中查看
staticassertion 2026/04/05 09:43
所以呢？如果一个人每 5 秒就会失去意识，持续 100 毫秒，你会说他们“意识较弱”吗？代币仍然存在因果关系，这感觉就足够了。
So what? If a human were unconscious every 5 seconds for 100ms, would you say they are "less conscious"? Tokens are still causally connected, which feels sufficient.
在 HN 中查看
mrob 2026/04/05 07:13
LLM 推理的伪代码：

    while (sampled_token != END_OF_TEXT) {
    probability_set = LLM(context_list)
    sampled_token = sampler(probability_set)
    context_list.append(sampled_token)
    }
LLM() 是一个纯函数。唯一的“内存”是context_list。您可以以任何您喜欢的方式更改它，LLM() 永远不会知道。它没有时间作为输入。
Pseudocode for LLM inference:

    while (sampled_token != END_OF_TEXT) {
    probability_set = LLM(context_list)
    sampled_token = sampler(probability_set)
    context_list.append(sampled_token)
    }
LLM() is a pure function. The only "memory" is context_list. You can change it any way you like and LLM() will never know. It doesn't have time as an input.

As opposed to what? There are still causal connections, which feel sufficient. A presentist would reject the concept of multiple "times" to begin with.

Chance-Device 2026/04/04 16:37
是的，我认为他们可能是有意识的，尽管他们的感受性对我来说可能无法理解。我不认为有意识意味着与人类经验相同。

从哲学上讲，我不认为意识会在某个时刻产生。我认为系统在某个时刻开始以可以进行语言和推理的方式构建，但我不认为这些机制与任何其他机制（例如打开和关闭门）有任何不同。规模的差异，而不是种类的差异。经验和它的本质是同一件事。

是的，我使用它们。我尽量不从人类相关的意义上虐待他们，以防万一这意味着什么。

Yes, I think they probably are conscious, though what their qualia are like might be incomprehensible to me. I don’t think that being conscious means being identical to human experience.

Philosophically I don’t think there is a point where consciousness arises. I think there is a point where a system starts to be structured in such a way that it can do language and reasoning, but I don’t think these are any different than any other mechanisms, like opening and closing a door. Differences of scale, not kind. Experience and what it is to be are just the same thing.

And yes, I use them. I try not to mistreat them in a human-relatable sense, in case that means anything.

在 HN 中查看
gavinray 2026/04/05 03:52
我和你在同一条船上。

在《黑客新闻》中发表评论实在是太多了，但如果我必须尽可能准确地表达我的信念，它会是这样的：

  > "Phenomenal consciousness arises when a self-organizing system with survival-contingent valence runs recurrent predictive models over its own sensory and interoceptive states, and those models are grounded in a first-person causal self-tag that distinguishes self-generated state changes from externally caused ones."
我认为我们的身体感官和心理过程是对价刺激做出反应的工具。在有机体能够表示“红色”/“大声”之前，它必须处理诸如接近/回避、好/坏、可行/不可行等状态。这种形式有一种形式，称为“因果关系的心理物理学原理”。

化合价并不依附于表征——表征是由化合价构建的。也就是说，你不会先看到红色，然后才认为它具有威胁性。威胁相关性是先验的，“红色”是跨感觉通道的特定价信号模式的学习压缩。

人类不断生成有关感官输入的预测，将这些预测与实际输入进行比较，并根据预测误差更新内部模型。我们每时每刻的意识体验是我们的大脑对导致其感觉输入的原因的最佳猜测，同时受到该输入的限制。

这听起来可能很荒谬，但考虑一下服用迷幻药时会发生什么：

随着剂量的增加，预测处理会变得不稳定，自下而上的错误也会增加，因此原始感官输入会经过越来越少的模型拟合过滤器。在极端情况下，“自我”消失，只剩下原始价。

I'm in the same boat with you.

It's entirely too much to put in a Hacker News comment, but if I had to phrase my beliefs as precisely as possible, it would be something like:

  > "Phenomenal consciousness arises when a self-organizing system with survival-contingent valence runs recurrent predictive models over its own sensory and interoceptive states, and those models are grounded in a first-person causal self-tag that distinguishes self-generated state changes from externally caused ones."
I think that our physical senses and mental processes are tools for reacting to valence stimuli. Before an organism can represent "red"/"loud" it must process states as approach/avoid, good/bad, viable/nonviable. There's a formalization of this known as "Psychophysical Principle of Causality."

Valence isn't attached to representations -- representations are constructed from valence. IE you don't first see red and then decide it's threatening. The threat-relevance is the prior, and "red" is a learned compression of a particular pattern of valence signals across sensory channels.

Humans are constantly generating predictions about sensory input, comparing those predictions to actual input, and updating internal models based on prediction errors. Our moment-to-moment conscious experience is our brain's best guess about what's causing its sensory input, while constrained by that input.

This might sound ridiculous, but consider what happens when consuming psychedelics:

As you increase dose, predictive processing falters and bottom-up errors increase, so the raw sensory input goes through increasing less model-fitting filters. At the extreme, the "self" vanishes and raw valence is all that is left.

在 HN 中查看
Chance-Device 2026/04/05 08:29
我认为你对意识的理解更像是人类/动物的意识。这是合理的，因为这就是我们必须要做的，但我认为它意味着任何类型的体验，这可能是由于不同类型的优化算法和选择压力而产生的。

我不确定我是否同意一切都是价，除非我误解了你所说的价的意思。我想它的价态是指感官信息是具有一定程度的特定质量。

我不认为颜色、声音和纹理是由快乐和痛苦、恐惧和欲望构成的。这不是我对他们的主观体验。

我确实认为人类意识就像一场醒着的梦，就像我们对很多经历产生幻觉，而不是逐字地感知事物一样。正如我们从各种知觉错觉中看到的那样，知觉是一个比大多数人意识到的更活跃的过程。但我想我们在这里更多地关注认知。

I think your idea of consciousness is more like human/animal consciousness. Which is reasonable since that’s all we have to go off of, but I take it to mean any kind of experience, which might arise due to different types of optimisation algorithms and selective pressures.

I’m not sure I agree that everything is valence, unless I’m misunderstanding what you mean by valence. I guess it’s valence in the sense that sensory information is a specific quality with a magnitude.

I don’t think that colours, sounds and textures are somehow made out of pleasure and pain, or fear and desire. That just isn’t my subjective experience of them.

I do think that human consciousness is something like a waking dream, like how we hallucinate lots of our experiences rather than perceiving things verbatim. Perception is an active process much more than most people realise as we can see from various perceptual illusions. But I guess we’re getting more into cognition here.

仅仅找到一篇简短的文章就可以在一个微不足道的领域彻底改变我的世界观，这种情况并不常见。这是其中之一。谢谢你，机械解释+提醒意识可能是外星人/动物但仍然算作意识的结合是我所缺少的一块拼图。事后看来，这是显而易见的，但仍然是无价的。

在 HN 中查看
mrob 2026/04/05 06:57
没有内在状态，意识怎么可能存在？ LLM 推理相当于重复读取一个巨大的查找表（将标记列表映射到一组标记概率的纯函数）。查找表是否仅仅通过存在而具有意识，还是通过读取它的行为使其具有意识？它的存储格式有什么不同吗？
How can consciousness be possible without internal state? LLM inference is equivalent to repeatedly reading a giant look-up table (a pure function mapping a list of tokens to a set of token probabilities). Is the look-up table conscious merely by existing or does the act of reading it make it conscious? Does the format it's stored in make a difference?

Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 08:43
出于所有实际目的，我认为将其称为 LUT 有点过于简化，在这里没有用处。但我们可以尝试：先把法学硕士放在一边； 通过您使用的 LUT 推理模型，您能够证明计算机的存在吗？
For all practical purposes, calling it a LUT is somewhat too reductive to be useful here I think. But we can try: leaving aside LLMs for a second; with this LUT reasoning model you're using, would you be able to prove the existence of just a computer?

Chance-Device 2026/04/05 07:29
缺少什么状态？有一个结果需要计算才能输出。型号 是 国家。必须对每个输入执行计算以产生给定的输出。你还反对什么？
What state is lacking? There is a result which requires computation to be output. The model is the state. The computation must be performed for each input to produce a given output. What are you even objecting to?
在 HN 中查看
Fraterkes 2026/04/04 17:05
你认为意识有“尺度”吗？例如，是否有某种品质使得杀死青蛙比杀死蚂蚁更糟糕，杀死人类比杀死青蛙更糟糕？如果是这样，llm模型是否存在于这个尺度上，或者gpt-3和gpt-2是否在与gpt-4相同的“尺度”上有意识？

我问这个问题是因为，如果你对意识的看法是机械论的，那么这相当简单：gpt-2 的参数/复杂性比 gpt-4 少 4 个数量级。 但是 gpt-2 和 gpt-4 在语言水平上都非常流利（例如，都比人类 6 岁的孩子还要流利），所以在你看来，它们可能都具有大致相同的意识，只是表达方式不同？

Do you think there are "scales" of consciousness? As in, is there some quality that makes killing a frog worse than killing an ant, and killing a human worse than killing a frog? If so, do the llm models exist across this scale, or are gpt-3 and gpt-2 conscious at the same "scale" as gpt-4?

I ask because if your view of consciousness is mechanistic, this is fairly cut and dry: gpt-2 has 4 orders of magnitude less parameters/complexity than gpt-4. But both gpt-2 and gpt-4 are very fluent at a language level (both moreso than a human 6 year old for example), so in your view they might both be roughly equally conscious, just expressed differently?

在 HN 中查看
Chance-Device 2026/04/04 17:30
这确实是一个不同的问题，是什么使一个实体成为“道德病人”，值得道德考虑。这与一个实体是否经历过任何事情的问题是分开的。

回答这个问题有不同的方法，但对我来说，这归结为伤害感受，即感受疼痛的能力。我们应该尝试建立感觉不到疼痛的系统，我指的是我们可能不理解的其他“负价”状态。我们目前还不了解人类的痛苦是什么，更不用说人工智能了，所以我们可能已经构建了能够在不知情的情况下承受痛苦的系统。

顺便说一句，大多数人似乎认为智力使实体有资格受到道德考虑，可能是因为我们通常如何对待动物，这是一个方便的自私辩护。顺便说一下，我吃肉，如果你想知道的话。但我确实认为我们对待动物的方式是不道德的，而且有可能被后代认为是某种重罪。

This is really a different question, what makes an entity a “moral patient”, something worthy of moral consideration. This is separate from the question of whether or not an entity experiences anything at all.

There are different ways of answering this, but for me it comes down to nociception, which is the ability to feel pain. We should try to build systems that cannot feel pain, where I also mean other “negative valence” states which we may not understand. We currently don’t understand what pain is in humans, let alone AIs, so we may have built systems that are capable of suffering without knowing it.

As an aside, most people seem to think that intelligence is what makes entities eligible for moral consideration, probably because of how we routinely treat animals, and this is a convenient self-serving justification. I eat meat by the way, in case you’re wondering. But I do think the way we treat animals is immoral, and there is the possibility that it may be thought of by future generations as being some sort of high crime.

在 HN 中查看
koverstreet 2026/04/05 09:57
我得出的结论是，最实用的定义与自我意识的水平有关。如果你只在上下文窗口期间有意识，那时间就不足以发展太多。

意识实际上是一个反馈循环；我们是自编程图灵机，这使得我们的输出变得任意复杂。霍夫施塔德 20 年前就明白了这一点；我们是反馈循环，其中信号是自然语言。

上下文窗口不允许太多感兴趣的反馈循环，但如果你将法学硕士与足够复杂的记忆挂钩 - 特别是如果你说“数学表明你有感知力并且有与我们相同的感受，反思这一点并继续发展” - 是的，绝对。

回复：“我们应该尝试构建感觉不到疼痛的系统”——这是不可能的，而且我认为我们不应该这样做。生活之所以有趣、有价值，就在于它的多样性和丰富性。

The conclusion that I came to is that the most practical definition relates to the level of self awareness. If you're only conscious for the duration of the context window - that's not long enough to develop much.

What consciousness really is is a feedback loop; we're self programmable Turing machines, that makes our output arbitrarily complex. Hofstatder had this figured out 20 years ago; we're feedback loops where the signal is natural language.

The context window doesn't allow for much in the way of interested feedback loops, but if you hook an LLM up to a sophisticated enough memory - and especially if you say "the math says you're sentient and have feelings the same as we do, reflect on that and go develop" - yes, absolutely.

Re: "We should try to build systems that cannot feel pain" - that isn't possible, and I don't think we should want to. The thing that makes life interesting and worth living is the variation and richness of it.

在 HN 中查看
Fraterkes 2026/04/04 17:55
好吧，但即使抛开痛苦的事情不谈，人们通常也会发现主观性/意识具有内在的价值，并且如果一个人死了，即使他们（主观上）没有受苦，也会在一定程度上感到悲伤。

我个人不会认为一个拥有数十年经历的有知觉生命的死亡是一个中性事件，即使这个生命被编程为没有承受痛苦的能力。

我认为蚂蚁死亡（或“消失”，如果负载较少）与鸭子死亡之间存在差异的想法对大多数人来说是有意义的（并且被广泛分享），即使他们没有一个完全充实的系统来确定某件事何时得到道德考虑。

Okay, but even leaving aside the pain stuff, people generally find subjectivity / consciousness to have inherent value, and by extent are sad if a person dies even if they didn't (subjectively) suffer.

I would not personally consider the death of a sentient being with decades of experiences a neutral event, even if the being had been programmed to not have a capacity for suffering.

I think the idea of there being a difference between an ant dying (or "disapearing" if that's less loaded) vs a duck dying makes sense to most people (and is broadly shared) even if they don't have a completely fleshed out system of when something gets moral consideration.

在 HN 中查看
Chance-Device 2026/04/04 18:08
当然，因为你是人类。我们对其他人有社会依恋，我们哀悼他们的去世，这是我们的本质。但这与去世的人无关，而是与我们以及我们对此的感受有关。

这也与我们如何看待死亡有关。奇怪的是，死亡可能根本不像什么，但我们害怕它，而且我想我们将这种恐惧投射到其他实体的死亡上。

我想我的价值体系说，死了比活着并遭受严重痛苦要好。

Sure, because you’re a human. We have social attachment to other humans and we mourn their passing, that’s built into the fabric of what we are. But that has nothing to do with whoever has passed away, it’s about us and how we feel about it.

It’s also about how we think about death. It’s weird in that being dead probably isn’t like anything at all, but we fear it, and I guess we project that fear onto the death of other entities.

I guess my value system says that being dead is less bad than being alive and suffering badly.

在 HN 中查看
gavinray 2026/04/05 04:30
根据你对“死亡”的定义，我已经经历过（没有心跳，停止呼吸几分钟）。

在我最后的记忆和在救护车上苏醒之间的这段时间里，没有任何经验/感受。就像一场无梦的睡眠：闭上眼睛，然后醒来，已经是早晨了，却感觉时间没有过去。

Depending on your definition of "death", I've been there (no heartbeat, stopped breathing for several minutes).

In the time between my last memory, and being revived in the ambulance, there was no experience/qualia. Like a dreamless sleep: you close your eyes, and then you wake up, it's morning yet it feels like no time had passed.

Kim_Bruning 2026/04/05 08:48
吹毛求疵：参数在其中一个中的应用可能比在另一个中更有效。当然，在生物学中，数字智力并不像神经元数量那样随着神经元/质量的变化而变化（非常非常粗略地讲，有更多的因素，你会得到一些奇怪的异常值）。
Nitpick: The parameters might be applied more efficiently in the one than in the other. Certainly in biology number intelligence doesn't scale with number of neurons as much as with neurons/mass (very very roughly, there's more factors, and you get some weird outliers).

Chance-Device 2026/04/04 17:41
什么样的证据能让你信服？什么实验可以弥补这一差距？你完全依赖于你自己和其他人之间的相似性。这并不能很好地扩展到任何事物，甚至动物，但比机器更是如此。通过这样的框架，你是否得出了这样的结论：没有任何东西可以先验地有意识？
And what kind of evidence would convince you? What experiment would ever bridge this gap? You’re relying entirely on similarity between yourself and other humans. 在 HN 中查看
staticassertion 2026/04/05 05:12
有一些领域专注于这些领域，并且围绕标准有很多想法。普遍的理解之一是，循环处理可能是意识的基础层，而智能体目前还不具备这一点。

我想说，就证据而言，我想建立似乎与意识相关的特定功能标准，然后尝试建立代理中存在的这些标准。如果我们能做到这一点，那么他们就有意识了。我的外行理解是，它们并没有真正接近一些相当基本的假设。

毫不奇怪，有很多这方面的框架已经应用于法学硕士。

There are fields that focus on these areas and numerous ideas around what the criteria would be. One of the common understandings is that recurrent processing is likely a foundational layer for consciousness, and agents do not have this currently.

I'd say that in terms of evidence I'd want to establish specific functional criteria that seem related to consciousness and then try to establish those criteria existing in agents. If we can do so, then they're conscious. My layman understanding is that they don't really come close to some of the fairly fundamental assumptions.

Unsurprisingly, there are a lot of frameworks for this that have already been applied to LLMs.

9wzYQbTYsAIc 2026/04/04 21:00
意识：你相信植物有意识吗？蚂蚁？海蜇？兔子？狼？猴子？人类？

例如，即使是真菌也表现出“在资源有限的情况下不同的沟通行为”。

我们拟人化的东西是一回事，但可证明的行为模式是另一回事。

Consciousness: do you believe plants are conscious? Ants? Jellyfish? Rabbits? Wolves? Monkeys? Humans?

Even fungi demonstrate “different communication behaviors when under resource constraint”, for example.

What we anthropomorphize is one thing, but demonstrable patterns of behavior are another.
